Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative relative clause plus passive verb phrase; subject prefix a-, negative ta-, locative implication of absence, passive -erwa.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who is absent avoids a direct danger, but may also miss what happens when people are present.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omuntu ataliyo tayingira omu kabi, kandi tasobora kubona omugisa oguliho.
Usage Contexts: Daily advice, meetings, family discipline, risk-taking, missed opportunities.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to homestead and forest imagery in Tooro oral teaching: a falling tree harms only those present, yet absence can also mean missing communal chances.
First Example Sentence: Araali obu ataiijire omu rusorrokano akahuka omugisa, abantu bagamba bati, Ataroho taterwa omuti.
Second Example Sentence: N'obwoba otiina, otasigara inyuma buli kiro; Ataroho taterwa omuti, baitu ataboona n'omugisa.
Third Example Sentence: Abakozi abatarabireyo ha mulimo tibafubirwe n'enjura, habwokuba Ataroho taterwa omuti.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominalized character-name idiom followed by a perfective/past verbal clause; compact narrative proverb with object noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone who refuses good advice eventually suffers the painful result of stubbornness.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omuntu atahurra obuhabuzi bw'abakuru aheza akagwa omu bizibu.
Usage Contexts: Moral teaching, youth guidance, caution after mistakes, family and school settings.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on a Tooro tale about an obstinate person who ignored warnings and ended up carrying a centipede, a painful symbol of consequences.
First Example Sentence: Tukakuhana kare, baitu okanga kuhurra; hati Nyantagambirwa akaheeka orunaku.
Second Example Sentence: Omwana obu yayangire ebigambo bya nyina, baamwijukya bati, Nyantagambirwa akaheeka orunaku.
Third Example Sentence: Omutendeki akagambira abaisiki ati, mutaleka obuhabuzi, Nyantagambirwa akaheeka orunaku.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed subject-verb clause with temporal complement and transitive verb; elliptical proverb contrasting delay with leftovers.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One who waits too long receives only the last, least valuable portion of an opportunity.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omuntu alinda muno ahika ha bintu ebisigaireho byonka.
Usage Contexts: Work deadlines, markets, farming seasons, education, opportunity-taking.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in food-sharing and animal-tail imagery: latecomers to communal distribution may receive the undesirable tail.
First Example Sentence: Abandi bakatandika kurima kare, Kule obu yaizire hanyuma abantu bagamba bati, Alinda kakyahweyo acumita omukira.
Second Example Sentence: Otaliinda akatale kuhwa; Alinda kakyahweyo acumita omukira.
Third Example Sentence: Orujumbura rutakahwa baingi bakatwara ebirungi, kandi alinda kakyahweyo acumita omukira.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound nickname plus perfective verbal clause; noun-class agreement with plural object and locative phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who insists on working alone becomes exhausted and wastes effort that should be shared.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omuntu akora wenka buli kintu atera kunanirwa; abantu basemeriire kukwatana.
Usage Contexts: Teamwork, farming, household labor, workplace planning, community mobilization.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Comes from banana-grove labor: tying or stripping many banana fibers alone is slow, tiring work in Tooro agrarian life.
First Example Sentence: Omukuru w'omulimo akagamba ati, Mboha-nyenka akamara ebigogo omurugonjo; mukwatane.
Second Example Sentence: Kakuba okora wenka buli kiro, Mboha-nyenka akamara ebigogo omurugonjo.
Third Example Sentence: Abakazi n'abasaija bakakora hamwe ha mbaga, habwokuba Mboha-nyenka akamara ebigogo omurugonjo.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Noun phrase subject with descriptive relative verb followed by negative habitual verb; animal-metaphor clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People who make loud threats often do not carry them out.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omuntu arayogerra muno n'ekiniga tasemeriire kukutiinisa atakakozire kintu.
Usage Contexts: Conflict, teasing, social criticism, schoolyard speech, political commentary.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses familiar village-dog behavior: a small dog may bark fiercely, while real danger is judged by action rather than noise.
First Example Sentence: Omutabani akagamba ebigambo by'amaani, baitu abantu bakamumanya bati, Akabwa kaboigora tikaruma.
Second Example Sentence: Otaatiina buli muntu arukuhakaniza n'eraka; Akabwa kaboigora tikaruma.
Third Example Sentence: Omu rukiiko bakaseka omusaija owatiinisize buli muntu, bati Akabwa kaboigora tikaruma.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Noun phrase with diminutive body-part noun plus adjective, followed by active habitual verb; metaphorical causation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A bad tongue causes harm; careless or malicious speech can damage people and relationships.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ebigambo bibi bisobora kuhutaaza abantu n'okusiisa obunywani.
Usage Contexts: Family mediation, public speaking, gossip, counseling, conflict prevention.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Tooro concern for respectful speech, where words are treated as socially powerful enough to bless, shame, or wound.
First Example Sentence: Akalimi kabi karoga; otagamba ebigambo ebihutaaza omutima gw'omuntu.
Second Example Sentence: Obu yagaambire kubi omu maka, nyina akamuhana ati, Akalimi kabi karoga.
Third Example Sentence: Omutekani w'orukiiko akatwijuukya ati, Akalimi kabi karoga kandi engambo nungi ezimbisa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Plural abstract noun subject with intransitive verb; compact metaphor comparing truth to a cutting instrument.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Truth can be painful, direct, or uncomfortable, but it reveals reality clearly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Amazima gasobora kusaasa, baitu gasoborra abantu ekiriho.
Usage Contexts: Mediation, leadership, accountability, testimony, difficult family conversations.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Part of moral discourse in Tooro homes and councils, where truth-telling is valued even when it embarrasses or hurts.
First Example Sentence: Amazima gasarra, baitu nigo gatukonyera kukora kurungi.
Second Example Sentence: Omukuru w'eka akamugambira amazima; Amazima gasarra, baitu tigaita.
Third Example Sentence: Obu twahikire ha kubara sente, Amazima gasarra kandi buli muntu akaculeera.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Plural noun subject with numeral modifier and infinitive verb phrase; cooperative-action proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Two hands accomplish care better than one; cooperation makes hard work easier.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Abantu babiri rundi baingi basobora kukora kurungi kukira omuntu omwe wenka.
Usage Contexts: Teamwork, childcare, farming, community service, household duties.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in child-care imagery: washing a child well requires both hands, mirroring Tooro values of shared responsibility.
First Example Sentence: Engaro ibiri kunaabisa ngana; tugabane omulimo gw'okurungiisa enju.
Second Example Sentence: Abaisiki n'aboojo bakakorra hamwe, habwokuba Engaro ibiri kunaabisa ngana.
Third Example Sentence: Omutwe gumu tigumara buli kintu; Engaro ibiri kunaabisa ngana.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical equative clause plus concessive subordinate clause; subject-verb agreement on gutaaha.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Good fortune can arrive unexpectedly, even when someone is not actively inviting it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Omugisa gusobora kuhika ha muntu atakugurindira rundi atakuguserra.
Usage Contexts: Hope, surprise success, gratitude, spiritual reflection, family encouragement.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses wind as an image of invisible movement through doors and homes, fitting Tooro speech about blessing, chance, and providence.
First Example Sentence: Omugisa mbeho, n'obu okinga gutaaha; Kansiime akatunga omulimo atarukulinda.
Second Example Sentence: Tuleke kwiha amaani; Omugisa mbeho, n'obu okinga gutaaha.
Third Example Sentence: Obu omugisa gwaizire omu ka, nyina akasema ati, Omugisa mbeho, n'obu okinga gutaaha.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Quantified noun phrase with possessive construction; elliptical locative proverb with animal symbol.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Every place, group, or situation has its own hidden danger, powerful person, or difficult custom.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Buli mwanya gugira ebizibu byagwo, abantu baagwo, n'emigisa yaagwo.
Usage Contexts: Travel, politics, workplace entry, clan relations, advice to newcomers.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes the hilly landscape of Tooro and the leopard as a symbol of local power, danger, and respect for unfamiliar territory.
First Example Sentence: Obu twahikire omu rubuga orusya, tata akatuhabura ati, Buli kasozi n'engo yako.
Second Example Sentence: Otasereka amaiso ha by'omwanya omusya; Buli kasozi n'engo yako.
Third Example Sentence: Abato ba Fort Portal bakamanya ngu Buli kasozi n'engo yako, kandi buli kicweka kigira empisa zaako.
